title: Tagine of lamb with prunes and almonds
subtitle: Moroccan slow-braised lamb with sweet spices, prunes, and toasted almonds.
excerpt: This Moroccan tagine is a slow braise of diced lamb shoulder in a spiced onion sauce, finished with prunes and a spoonful of honey. The almonds go in at the end, toasted golden, and provide a contrast to the soft, sweet sauce. Two hours of gentle simmering and almost no active cooking.
sourceNotes: A Moroccan tagine of the mrouzia tradition, combining meat with dried fruit and sweet spices. Documented across Moroccan culinary literature of the twentieth century. The lamb-and-prune combination is most closely associated with the Fez region, where it appears at celebrations and family feasts. The combination of cinnamon, ginger, and honey with lamb predates Islamic-era cooking in North Africa and reflects Berber culinary traditions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the finished dish without the almonds for up to three months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently. Add freshly toasted almonds on serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well in a large casserole. The braise is not sensitive to quantity.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tagine improves made a day ahead. Cool, refrigerate, and reheat gently with a splash of water if needed. Add the honey and almonds when reheating.

recipeTools.notes: Heavy casserole or large sauté pan with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron is ideal.

content.text: Moroccan lamb tagines with fruit are not sweet in the way that a dessert is sweet. The prunes add body and a deep, jammy note; the honey is present but restrained; and the spices (cinnamon, ginger, saffron in some versions) keep the sauce complex rather than cloying. The lamb shoulder provides enough collagen to make the sauce silky over a long braise without any thickening agent.

The active cooking here is minimal. The onion softens with the spices first, then the lamb goes in. From that point, the dish largely looks after itself for two hours over a low flame. The prunes and honey are added in the final thirty minutes so the prunes soften without disintegrating.

Almonds go in at the very end, toasted separately. Raw almonds added to the braise would turn soft; toasted and added at the last moment, they give a crunch that cuts through the richness of the sauce. Couscous is the obvious accompaniment.

items.prepNote: blanched, toasted in a dry pan

content.text: Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} lamb pieces generously with salt and pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large, heavy pan over medium-high heat. Working in batches to avoid crowding, brown the lamb on all sides for three to four minutes per batch. Transfer to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} sliced onions to the pan and cook for ten minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened and golden. Add the {{garlic}} garlic, cinnamon, ginger, and turmeric, and cook for one minute until fragrant.

Return the lamb to the pan. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of stock. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with a tight-fitting lid, and cook over low heat for one and a half hours until the lamb is very tender. Check occasionally and add a splash of water if the liquid drops below the level of the lamb.

Add {{prunes}} of pitted prunes and {{honey}} of honey to the pan. Stir gently to combine. Cover and continue cooking for thirty minutes until the prunes have softened and the sauce has reduced to a glossy, thick coating. Taste and adjust salt.
Toast the {{almonds}} almonds in a dry frying pan over medium heat for three to four minutes, shaking the pan, until golden. Scatter over the tagine just before serving. Serve with couscous.

items.symptom: The sauce is thin after two hours
items.cause: Too much liquid was added, or the lid was not on tight.
items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat to medium. Simmer uncovered for ten to fifteen minutes until the sauce reduces to a syrupy consistency.

items.cause: They were added too early, or the heat was too high.
items.fix: For a cleaner result, simmer the prunes separately in a little water for twenty minutes before adding. Next time, add them for the last thirty minutes only.

items.symptom: The lamb is still tough after two hours
items.cause: The heat was too low, or the lamb pieces were too large.
items.fix: Continue cooking for a further thirty minutes. Shoulder becomes tender with time — it cannot be rushed, but it will get there.

content.text: The combination of lamb with sweet spices and dried fruit is one of the oldest preparations in North African and Middle Eastern cooking. In Morocco it is most associated with the mrouzia — a festive dish eaten after Eid al-Adha, when lamb is abundant and the cooking is done slowly over a full day. The everyday version, cooked with prunes and honey over two hours rather than four or five, is a home staple particularly in the cities of Fez and Marrakech. The almonds are not decoration: they are a structural part of the dish, providing texture that the slow-cooked sauce and fruit cannot.
